entry.title: Tools for rhythmicity detection
entry.content:
Introduction

In an early period, some tools can only detect rhythmicity, i.e. to tell you whether this gene or transcript or protein had cycling expression, and they cannot tell you that whether the features you tested had different amplitudes, phases, etc. in different conditions. Thus, some tools were later created for doing that and they called this process “differential rhythmicity“.

There are two main types of methods, parametric and nonparametric. The difference between these two is just whether based on a known mathematical model or not.

Parametric methods are able to detect weaker rhythms than nonparametric methods. However, parametric methods rely on stricter assumptions: often, measurement errors must have Gaussian distributions, and the methods are sensible to outliers.

Nonparametric methods sacrifice some degree of power in order to gain robustness against outliers, and they make fewer assumptions about measurement errors.

Summary

Rhythmicity Detection

Type Method Year Notes Others
Parametric Fisher’s G test 2004 Exact test with g-statistics Fourier analysis
Sinusoidal regression 2013 Curve fitting
Cosinor regression 2014 Curve fitting
Nonparametric JTK_CYCLE 2010 JT test + Kendall's tau correlations Symmetric rhythms only
RAIN 2014 Extend JTK to include asymmetric rhythms

Differential Rhythmicity

Type Method Year Notes Others
Parametric DODR 2016 Detects changes in periodic paramters 2 conditions only
Nonparametric LimoRhyde 2018 Combination of cosinor regression and limma Allows multiple experimental variables

Details

Appear soon!
entry.summary:
Introduction

In an early period, some tools

entry.title: Implementations of tissue-aware normalization for RNA-seq
entry.content:
In this post I’ll show some GTEx expresssion data using the normalization methods introduced in my last post: Normalization methods for RNA-seq.

Actually I was suggested to compare all these methods before doing DE analylsis by Joey, my advisor :P, I thought just using qsmooth was OK, but we finally ended up with tissue-aware quantile normalization.

Filter Genes

I am manipulating GTEx v8 data, so I first filtered lowly-expressed genes after some prepossesses:

Compare Normalization

Normalize selected samples in tissue-aware way.

  1. TMM: trimmed mean of M-values
  2. RLE: relative log expression
  3. UQ: upper quartile
  4. DESeq: median-ratio-scaling
  5. QN: quantile normalization
  6. qsmooth: smooth quantile normalization
  7. none: original data

The following is a simple function to perform all of these, extended the one in yarn.

Visualize distributions

I chose some tissues to see their distribution.

Some helper functions:

Present the distributions:

I don’t know why there are still many lowly-expressed genes in some of these tissues, testis looks good though. The first three - TMM, RLE, UQ - are quite similar; DESeq method seems the same as none… I don’t know how to determine which is better for QN and qsmooth, I need to clarify the maths first… QAQ

Discussion of QN and qsmooth

I have been confused about the differences between doing qsmooth for the whole dataset and doing quantile normalization in each tissue (tissue-aware QN) since I read the YARN and qsmooth paper. In the qsmooth paper, the authors stated that advantages of qsmooth include:

  1. preservation of global differences in distributions corresponding to different biological conditions
  2. non-reliance on external information
  3. applicability to many different high-throughput technologies
  4. the return of normalized data that can be used for many types of downstream analyses

For the first and second ones, I think these are related to the weights assigned to each quantile when doing qsmooth, so the tissue-aware quantile normalization cannot keep global differences? Then how to explain this difference (from my plots for example..)?

Merry Xmas!!             /`;--._`''--._O'@;            /&*,()~o`;-.,_ `""`) *          /`,@ ;+& () o*`;-';\           (`""--.,_0 +% @' &()\           /-.,_    ``''--....-'`)  *      *    /@%;o`:;'--,.__   __.'\          ;*,&(); @ % &^;~`"`o;@();         *          /(); o^~; & ().o@*&`;&%O\          `"="==""==,,,.,="=="==="`       __.----.(\-''#####---...___...-----._     '`         \)_`"""""`             .--' ')           o(  )_-\             `"""` `
entry.summary: In this post I’ll show some GTEx expresssion data using the normalization methods introduced in my last post:

entry.title: Normalization methods for RNA-seq
entry.content:
Why normalization?

I usually make the reasons for every implementation or analysis clear and this is much more important than knowing how to do something.

The reason to do normalization is very simple: to FAIRLY compare the expression values within or between samples. In this article, the author explained quite well, however I found something wrong in the table of normalization methods, so be careful! I have mentioned a bit in my first RNA-seq post, I would summarize them here though. Mainly, these are factors needed to be considered (images were borrowed from that post and this post):

Within sample:

  • Gene length: longer gene would have more counts

  • GC-content: GC-rich and GC-poor fragments tend to be under-represented

  • Sequence position: due to RNA degradation, I am still not sure about it, explain it later

Between sample:

  • Sequencing depth: the total amout of RNA varies in each sample

  • RNA composition: maybe some sample-specific genes highly expressed or contamination

Normalization methods

Here I only focus on some methods commonly seen everywhere, mostly to address between-sample bias.

Total Count

I saw a paper saying that TC, just CPM I think, is a between-sample normalization. It makes some sense because the CPM value has been divided by the total amount of sequenced fragments, so they are comparable to some extent. However, it’s still not a good between-sample comparison and better using appropriate statistics to infer DEGs.

Upper Quartile Normalization

UQ is like dividing a scaling factor, which is the 75th percentile value (upper quartile). This is a blog talking about it: FPKM/RPKM normalization caveat and upper quartile normalization

Trimmed Mean of M-values Normalization

TMM normalization is quite widly used. It’s to choose a sample as a reference sample, then calculate fold changes and absolute expression levels relative to that sample. The genes are trimmed twice by these two values, to remove differentially expressed genes, then the trimmed mean of the fold changes is found for each sample. Read counts are scaled by this trimmed mean and the total count of their sample (C. Evans et al, arxiv 2016). The “M-values” means log expression ratios.

I don’t quite understand the details, but it’s just scaling like others. Need some time to understand this paper: A scaling normalization method for differential expression analysis of RNA-seq data. BTW, I took a look at the function calcNormFactors, which can implement TMM, RLE, upperqurtile, or none.

DESeq Median Of Ratios Normalization

This strategy is to find a size factor for each sample, such that the ratios of size factors of different samples represent the ratio of their respective sequencing depths. The size factor of a sample is the median of ratios of each read count to the geometric mean of all read counts for that gene across all samples. Refer to this paper. The method is straitforwardly explained here (again): Introduction to DGE.

Median Ratio Normalization

It’s not the DESeq method and is similar to TMM but more robust. Read counts are divided by the total count of their sample, then averaged across all samples in a condition for a given gene. This produces an average count-normalized value for each gene and each condition, and the median of the ratios of these values between conditions is taken. The original counts are then normalized by this median and their library size. Refer to this paper.

Quantile Normalization

Quantile normalization is to make two distribution same, one reference one target. If there are more distributions and without a reference distribution, sort as before, then set to the average of the distributions.

Here are two clear explainations: 1) Quantile normalisation in R; 2) StatQuest: Quantile Normalization.

Smooth Quantile Normalization

This is the QN with a relaxed assumption, allowing that samples in different groups have different distributions. It’s also called qsmooth. Details see this paper: Smooth quantile normalization.

The next article will be implementaions in R.
entry.summary:
Why normalization?

I usuall

entry.title: FW: 美國研究助理申請：大學生找美國研究助理全攻略

戳進去看詳細的申請過程和 tips 喔 -> Cumulonimbus☁ - 美國研究助理申請：大學生找美國研究助理全攻略

entry.title: A lunch conversation
entry.content:
Today Silvia treated me a lunch, since I am gonna leave US soon.

As a start, I mention about my trip to New York City and Philadelphia. I say I saw so many religious sculptures and pictures in Metropolitan Museum at NYC, while I don’t quite understand meanings behind them. Then Silvia starts to tell me the stories about Jesus and beliefs of them because she is a Christian.

I’ll briefly describe what she says. We talk in Chinese so I don’t know if I use the words or jargons correctly. The world we live was created by God, and the Blessed Trinity - the Father, the Son (Jesus Christ), and the Holy Spirit - are alternities of this one God. Jesus was born to save everyone (Christian only) from the penalty of sin by his death, as everyone will have many sins all over lifetime. What is sin? In her view, she belives that not only the bad actions that break laws but also the impulse to hit/kill someone or the time that one cannot control him/herself to do something which should not be done or something like that. Therefore, if you choose to become an Christian, you will go the heaven after death because you can recover the relationship between you and the God (it means save the sins), otherwise you just go to hell…

I am a little bit suprise they Christians firmly believe that human beings are created by the God (not by evolution) but she agrees with the micro evolution. That’s so strange because I think what moves forward micro evolution also promotes macro evolution. Every creature starts from the very first-appeared DNAs, and that “selfish” DNA assembled more protein complexs and stucures forming a cell then more and more complicated organs and finally an organism, all these are to duplicate themselves so that they (and their offsprings) can continue to exist in this world. And “selfish” is exactly the reason of evolution, no matter micro or macro.

What’s more important, there are so many ideas from Christians are same as those I acquire/think from my life experience. For example, everything happening in this world is pre-arranged already, many events are not controlled by yourself but something else. Thus I think this “something else” in my mind is equal to the God that Christians believe. Also, Silvia says the arragements for everyone composes the best route of life because of the love from the God. That’s what I trust as well - every creature will find their own way - no matter how many time lines or parallel worlds, the one I am in is the best. Well, they can treat the essential events, such as PhD application or something deadly important peacefully, this is the thing I need to learn, maybe I am still too young.

Finally I ask Silvia the opinion of Christian about human’s consciousness. She replys me by repeating the concepts stated before: the God loves people. Therefore he gives people their own “free will”, rather than making people like something running under codes, and following some rules. And freedom is not letting you do whatever you wanna but not to do the things you don’t wanna do. She argues that the freedom is from saving the sins with the help of God, and it (saving sins, getting free?) is also the main purpose of a person living in the world. Hmmm.. at the moment I think I just want to find out what on earth your GOD is and why the world is as it is, that’s the purpose for me to live on the earth.

This is the first time I have a general overview of the religion, before that I know religions are about the wise summary of life experience and some answers of questions about human beings and this universe, oh I will definitely read more religious books.
entry.summary:
Today Silvia treated me a lunch, since I am gonna leave US soon.

As a start, I mention about my trip to New York City and Philadel

entry.title: Basics of Bootstrapping
entry.content:
What Is Bootstrapping

Note that bootstrapping has different meanings in computing and statistics.

Bootstrapping is actually resampling with replacement.

Here is a simple example. We begin with a statistical sample from a population that we know nothing about. Our goal will be a 90% confidence interval about the mean of the sample.

The sample data I have is 3, 5, 7, 18, 10, then we could generate bootstrap samples like:

  • 7, 3, 3, 10, 5
  • 18, 18, 10, 3, 7
  • 3, 10, 7, 5, 10
  • 3, 3, 18, 7, 5
  • 5, 7, 7, 7, 10
  • 10, 3, 7, 10, 18
  • …

Now that we have these samples, the means of our bootstrap samples can be calculated. Finally, we can estimate the confidence interval (95th and 5th percentiles) of our population mean.

To sum up, through bootstrapping you are simply taking samples over and over again from the same group of data (your sample data) to provide an estimate of the sampling distribution of the sample statistic in question, rather than provide an estimate of the population distribution – we take our sample itself as a model of the population.

When Could Use Bootstrapping

It is especially useful when the sample size that we are working with is small. Under usual circumstances, sample sizes of less than 40 (30?) cannot be dealt with by assuming a normal distribution or a t distribution. Bootstrap techniques work quite well with samples that have less than 40 elements. The reason for this is that bootstrapping involves resampling. These kinds of techniques assume nothing about the distribution of our data.

Real Example

References:

  1. ThoughtCo - Example of Bootstrapping

  2. StackExchange - Explaining to laypeople why bootstrapping works
entry.summary: What Is Bootstrapping</
